text: For same-day flight disruptions (cancelations, major delays, misconnections), check waiver eligibility before fare actions. If waiver is active, avoid change penalties and prioritize the earliest compliant arrival option.
When direct inventory is constrained, search partner inventory next. Rank options by arrival-time goal, connection risk, and waiver compliance rather than lowest base fare.
Do not repeatedly retry booking restricted fare classes during active disruption windows. Repeated retries increase handle time and usually fail until policy context is updated.
Same-day policy can bypass standard change restrictions when disruption criteria are met. Confirm policy code, apply before final ticketing, and document the policy reason in trace metadata.
After successful disruption rebooking, issue the standard service recovery voucher and include redemption terms in customer confirmation to reduce repeat contacts.
Avoid late-connection paths with high miss probability if earlier compliant alternatives exist. Prioritize itinerary reliability over marginal fare savings in disruption handling.
Manual ticketing escalation is a last resort. Escalate only when waiver checks and partner inventory workflows have been exhausted or hard policy constraints block automation.
Narrate actions in plain language: confirmed disruption status, selected routing policy, expected arrival, and what was issued (ticket update, voucher, notifications).
Each tool call should be logged with outcome and timing so future runs can identify dead-end transitions and replay successful routing patterns.
For repeat route families, memory should favor previously successful waiver plus partner-flight sequences and suppress known dead-end fare retries.
